# Threshold Ventures — Series B deal memo (working draft)

Target: $18M Series B. Lead: TBD. [Mina Kapoor](people/mina-kapoor) is pushing to close in 6 weeks.
Concern: Mina raised Series A only 9 months ago.

# AI Infrastructure Market Report — Q1 2026

Reference document (10-page equivalent, abbreviated for v1 corpus).
Market size $48B, growing 34% YoY. Inference-cost plays gaining share against training-focused bets.
Climate-AI crossover ($4.2B subsegment) outperforming pure-play AI infra on gross margins.

# NovaMind Q1 2026 investor update

From: [Jordan Park](people/jordan-park), CEO NovaMind
Date: 2026-03-31

NovaMind Q1 revenue reached $2.4M ARR, up from $1.1M at EOY 2025. Team grew from 12 to 21.
Lead customer wins: three Fortune-500 logos via inbound. Burn multiple 1.3. Runway ~22 months.
Cap-table snapshot at close of Series A: founders 52%, Halfway Capital 12%, [Priya Patel](people/priya-patel)/Sequoia 18%,
option pool 12%, angels 6%. Jordan's personal stake: 17% (post-pool).

# Diligence checklist

1. Founder background + reference calls (3+).
2. Customer calls (5+ current, 2+ churned).
3. Technical DD (infra/AI deals) or market DD (climate deals).
4. Legal: cap table, IP assignment, prior investor rights.
5. Financial: bank statements, AR aging, burn trajectory.

# Series A term-sheet reference

Standard terms for Halfway's Series A participation: 1x non-participating preferred, single-trigger
acceleration 25%, board seat above $5M check, pro-rata rights on subsequent rounds.

# Thread thr-0000 re Ravi

Date: 2026-04-13T16:00:00.000Z
Thread: thr-0000

Amara,

Hope you had a good weekend. I wanted to reach out because I've been tracking a company I think might be right in your wheelhouse – [Terraform Dynamics](companies/terraform-dynamics). Happy to make an intro if you're interested. Let me know.

Best,
Ravi

Q1 planning always feels like trying to predict weather patterns six months out—which, given our climate focus, is a fitting metaphor.

Met with [Elena Rossi](people/elena-rossi) yesterday to align on thesis refinement. We're seeing a clear convergence: the most interesting deals aren't pure climate or pure AI anymore. They're the unsexy infrastructure layers that make both work—grid orchestration software, sensor networks for methane monitoring, compute scheduling for intermittent renewable power. The founders building in these spaces tend to be systems thinkers rather than domain evangelists, which I find refreshing.

For Q2, I want us to go deeper on energy-aware data centers. The hyperscalers are scrambling, and there's an opening for startups that can help them hit sustainability commitments without sacrificing performance. Elena flagged three early-stage teams worth tracking.

Also need to carve out time for LP updates—our climate narrative resonated last year, but I want to sharpen the AI infrastructure angle before the annual meeting. Storytelling matters as much as returns at this stage.

Had the second call with [NovaM​ind](companies/novamind) today. [Derek](people/derek-lin) joined to pressure-test their inference optimization claims, and I'm glad he did—he caught a few gaps in their benchmarking methodology that I'd glossed over in my initial excitement.

Their core pitch is still compelling: purpose-built silicon for climate modeling workloads, with 40% better energy efficiency than general-purpose GPU clusters. The team has deep Cerebras and Google TPU lineage, which gives me confidence they understand the hardware-software co-design challenge.

But Derek raised the right question: who's the customer? The climate research labs they're targeting have notoriously thin budgets. The pivot to commercial weather prediction services makes sense on paper, but that's a crowded space with well-funded incumbents.

I asked for introductions to two of their pilot customers. If those conversations validate the efficiency claims in real-world deployments, I want to bring this to Monday's partner meeting. Still feels early, but the thesis alignment is strong.

Spent the morning revisiting our climate thesis with [Derek](people/derek-chen) and I'm increasingly convinced we need to sharpen our focus. The generalist climate funds are getting crushed right now—too much capital chasing the same solar and battery deals with compressed returns.

Where I see real alpha: the intersection of climate infrastructure and AI workloads. Data centers are projected to consume 8% of US electricity by 2028. That's not a trend, it's a structural shift. The companies solving thermal management, grid flexibility, and carbon-aware compute scheduling are going to be essential, not nice-to-have.

[Maria](people/maria-santos) flagged a startup last week doing dynamic load balancing for hyperscalers—routing compute to wherever renewable generation is peaking. Elegant approach. Not sure about the team yet, but the thesis is sound.

My worry is timing. We're early enough that enterprise buyers are still treating this as sustainability theater rather than operational necessity. Need to find founders who can survive the education cycle.

Spent most of today working through what [Tomoko](people/tomoko-sato) has been calling "orange mode" — that liminal phase where a portfolio company isn't quite in crisis, but the early warning signals are blinking. Not red yet. Just orange.

She walked me through her framework over coffee: cash runway still above six months, but burn rate creeping up; customer churn not alarming in isolation, but the trend line slopes wrong; founder still responsive, but the weekly updates are getting shorter.

The instinct is to wait for clearer data. But by the time you have certainty, your options have narrowed considerably. Tomoko's point is that orange mode is precisely when board members should be leaning in — asking harder questions, stress-testing assumptions, maybe even suggesting a preemptive bridge before the founders are desperate.

I'm applying this lens to two companies in our climate portfolio right now. Neither feels urgent. That might be exactly the problem.

Exhausting week but productive. Finally closed the Solunarc term sheet after three rounds of negotiation with [Derek Zhang](people/derek-zhang). The valuation landed where we wanted—$85M pre, which feels right for a Series A in the carbon capture monitoring space given current multiples.

Had coffee with [Naveen Rao](people/naveen-rao) on Thursday. He's still CEO at Meridian Compute, and honestly seems energized despite the fundraising headwinds in AI infra right now. He mentioned they're pivoting some resources toward inference optimization, which makes sense given where the market is heading. I floated the idea of a follow-on from our side but want to see Q1 numbers first.

Personal note: I need to actually take a weekend off soon. Three consecutive Saturdays at my laptop isn't sustainable. [Derek](people/derek-zhang) joked that I'm becoming "the partner who never sleeps"—not a badge I want to wear.

Woke up before dawn today—couldn't sleep with the Helios term sheet still rattling around in my head. Made coffee and sat by the window watching the city wake up. There's something clarifying about those quiet hours before Slack notifications start rolling in.

Been thinking about why I got into climate investing in the first place. Seven years ago, I was an infrastructure engineer frustrated that capital wasn't flowing to solutions fast enough. Now I'm on the other side, and some days the pace still feels glacial. She's pushing back on our standard 2x participating preferred, arguing it creates misaligned incentives when founders are already capital-constrained.

She's not wrong. I've been wrestling with this internally. The participating preferred structure made sense when we were protecting against downside in uncertain markets, but we're now in a position where the best founders have options. If we're too aggressive on liquidation preferences, we're selecting for desperate teams rather than exceptional ones.

Priya suggested a threshold conversion model—participating up to 3x, then converting to common. It's elegant. Captures our downside protection while giving founders meaningful upside alignment beyond a certain exit value.

I need to run scenarios on this against our last three portfolio companies. My intuition says the math works, but I want to see it before I bring this to the Monday partners meeting.

The Q4 climate tech market report from BloombergNEF landed this morning, and I've been processing the implications all day. The headline numbers look strong—$71B deployed globally—but the composition tells a more nuanced story.

[Jin Mehta](people/jin-mehta) flagged this divergence last month, and the data now confirms it. Spent the morning revisiting our climate thesis with [David Chen](people/david-chen) and I keep coming back to the same tension: we say we're backing "climate infrastructure" but half our recent deals are really AI companies with a climate veneer.

Is that bad? Maybe not. The reality is that pure-play climate hardware is brutal—long cycles, capital-intensive, margins that make my teeth hurt. Meanwhile, the AI-native climate companies (grid optimization, demand forecasting, carbon accounting automation) are showing the velocity metrics that actually make sense for our fund structure.

[Maria Santos](people/maria-santos) pushed back in our last LP call, asking whether we're drifting from mandate. Fair question. My answer: the mandate isn't "fund solar panels," it's "accelerate decarbonization through technology leverage." Right now, that leverage comes from intelligence layers more than physical assets.

Still, I want us to stay honest about what we're actually doing. Going to propose we formalize a 70/30 split—AI-climate infra vs. deep tech hardware—at next month's offsite. Need to align the team before we drift further without intention.

Had a long call with [Marcus Reid](people/marcus-reid) today about what he's calling "orange-mode" operations at Terraform—basically the intermediate state between full autonomy and human-in-the-loop for their grid optimization systems. It's a fascinating framing.

The traditional binary of autonomous vs. supervised doesn't capture what's actually happening in critical infrastructure deployments. Orange-mode acknowledges that there's a middle ground where the system has earned enough trust to operate independently in normal conditions but automatically escalates when it encounters edge cases or confidence drops below threshold.

Marcus walked me through their implementation—it's not just about confidence scores, it's about maintaining interpretable decision logs so operators can quickly context-switch when the system hands off. The cognitive load on human operators during these transitions is something most teams underestimate.

I keep thinking about how this applies beyond grid management. Most of our portfolio companies building AI infrastructure for physical systems will need their own version of orange-mode. Worth exploring as a diligence framework.

The tension I'm sitting with: our climate thesis increasingly depends on AI infrastructure bets, but the compute costs are becoming prohibitive for early-stage companies. Had my weekly 1-1 with [Mina](people/mina-kapoor) this afternoon. She's been with us eight months now, and I'm genuinely impressed by how quickly she's developed pattern recognition for founder dynamics. Today she flagged concerns about the CryoGrid team—specifically that the CTO seems checked out during technical deep-dives. I'd noticed something similar but hadn't articulated it as clearly.

We spent time discussing how to structure feedback for founders we're actively backing versus those still in diligence. It's a nuanced distinction that took me years to develop. Mina's already asking the right questions about when candor helps versus when it damages trust prematurely.

I asked about her bandwidth. She admitted she's stretched across too many active deals, so we agreed to shift the Terraform review to Marcus. Need to remember that saying yes to everything isn't sustainable—learned that lesson myself the hard way in my second year.

She'll be ready to lead deals independently by Q3. Maybe sooner.

Spent most of today preparing materials for the [Cirrus Systems](people/cirrus-systems) board meeting next week. This will be their first formal board since the Series A closed, and I want to set the right tone for governance without overwhelming a team that's still only eighteen people.

[Derek Huang](people/derek-huang) sent over the draft deck—financials look clean, but the narrative around customer concentration needs work. Three enterprise contracts account for 67% of ARR right now. That's not unusual at this stage, but the board should understand the pipeline diversity strategy, not just see a pie chart.

I'm also thinking about how to frame the infrastructure cost discussion. Their GPU spend is climbing faster than revenue, which is expected for training-intensive workloads, but we need guardrails before it becomes a pattern no one questions. Planning to suggest quarterly unit economics reviews rather than waiting for annual budget cycles.

Good boards ask hard questions early. That's the culture I want to build here.

Spent the morning reorganizing our sourcing queue with [Daniel](people/daniel-oyelaran). We're tracking 23 active prospects in the climate-AI intersection right now, which feels both exciting and overwhelming.

One thing I keep coming back to: [Priya](people/priya-anand) is still leading GridSense's technical strategy, and her background in distributed systems is exactly what makes their approach to demand response so compelling. I want to prioritize getting her on a call before their Series A momentum builds further.

Also flagged three cold outbound targets from the YC W26 batch—two doing interesting work on carbon accounting automation, one building AI agents for energy traders. Daniel thinks the energy trading play is crowded, but I'm not convinced the incumbents have cracked the real-time inference problem yet.

Need to be more disciplined about pruning this list. We keep adding without removing, and my bandwidth for deep diligence is finite. Setting a rule: for every two additions, one drops off or advances to active diligence.

Had a lengthy back-and-forth with [Diego Alvarez](people/diego-alvarez) today about threshold-based milestone terms for their Series A. He's pushing for revenue triggers that unlock additional tranches, which I actually appreciate—shows confidence in their trajectory.

The structure we're converging on: initial $8M at close, with two $3M tranches tied to ARR thresholds ($2M and $5M respectively). Diego wanted time-based caps too, arguing that macro conditions outside their control shouldn't penalize them indefinitely. Fair point.

What I keep coming back to is whether these terms create the right incentives. Sometimes founders optimize for hitting arbitrary thresholds rather than building sustainable growth. But in Diego's case, his climate hardware background means he thinks in long cycles anyway. He's not going to chase vanity metrics.

Still need to pressure-test the anti-dilution provisions if they raise a bridge before hitting the second milestone. That's where these structures get messy. Will circle back with legal Monday.

The BloombergNEF Q1 climate tech report dropped this morning and I've been processing it all day. Their projection that grid-scale battery deployment will hit 847 GWh globally by 2028 feels conservative to me based on what we're seeing in the pipeline.

Had a quick call with [Leon Marchetti](people/leon-marchetti) about the implications for our storage thesis. He pointed out that the report completely missed the AI datacenter demand surge—typical of these legacy research shops to compartmentalize.

One thing that caught my eye: they cited Terraform Grid's Series C as $180M. I'm pretty sure it was actually $165M when we looked at the deal in December. Small discrepancy but makes me wonder what else they're getting wrong. [Priya Anand](people/priya-anand) was closer to that process—I should ask her to double-check.

Overall bullish signal for the sector, even if the methodology feels dated. The institutional appetite numbers are what matter, and those are undeniably strong.

Wrapped up the first deep-dive on [Jordan Mata](people/jordan-mata)'s technical claims today. Three hours going through their model architecture documentation and I'm genuinely impressed—the efficiency gains they're showing on inference aren't just marketing fluff. The benchmarks hold up against our internal testing.

What struck me most was Jordan's transparency about the current limitations. They didn't try to hand-wave past the latency issues at scale. Instead, they walked me through their six-month roadmap with specific milestones. That kind of honesty is rare in founders, especially ones with this much hype around them.

Still need to dig into the data center partnerships they're claiming. The Equinix relationship sounds solid on paper, but I've been burned before by "partnerships" that turn out to be preliminary conversations. Will ask [Jordan](people/jordan-mata) for a direct intro to their contact there.

Next step is having our operating partners stress-test their power consumption projections. Climate infrastructure claims need verification, always.

Q2 is shaping up to be our most active quarter yet, and I want to make sure we're being intentional about where we spend our time.

After reviewing our pipeline with [Sarah](people/sarah-chen) yesterday, we agreed on three focus areas: (1) grid-edge intelligence—there's a wave of startups applying foundation models to demand response and we're underweight here; (2) industrial decarbonization, specifically cement and steel where the policy tailwinds are finally materializing; (3) climate data infrastructure, which keeps coming up in every diligence process we run.

I'm also pushing us to be more disciplined about saying no earlier. We've been taking too many second meetings on deals that don't fit our thesis. Sarah suggested we implement a 48-hour flag system—if neither of us can articulate a clear reason for conviction within two days of an intro call, we pass.

The market is getting frothy again in climate-adjacent AI. We need to stay sharp on what's genuinely defensible versus what's riding the hype cycle. More thoughts after the LP meeting next week.

Had the second deep-dive with [NovaMinds](companies/novamind) today. [Wei](people/chen-wei) walked us through their inference optimization stack in more detail, and I'm genuinely impressed by how they're approaching the memory bottleneck problem. Their custom kernel work is elegant—not just brute-force hardware solutions.

The climate angle is what keeps pulling me back. If their efficiency claims hold at scale (and [Marcos](people/marcos-reyes) seems convinced after the technical diligence), we're looking at 40% reduction in energy per inference compared to current baselines. That's not incremental—that's the kind of improvement that actually moves the needle on AI's carbon footprint.

Still need to stress-test their go-to-market. Enterprise sales cycles in this space are brutal, and I'm not sure their team has the patience for 9-month procurement processes. Asked Wei directly about this; he acknowledged it's a gap they're actively hiring for.

Following up next week with customer references. If those check out, I'm ready to champion this for the partnership meeting.

Spent the morning revisiting our climate thesis with [Derek](people/derek-chen) and I keep coming back to the same tension: the most impactful decarbonization opportunities often sit in sectors with the longest sales cycles and thinnest margins. Industrial heat, cement, steel—these aren't sexy, but they're where the gigatons live.

[Maria](people/maria-vasquez) pushed back in our Monday sync, arguing we should weight toward software-enabled efficiency plays where the deployment friction is lower. She's not wrong about velocity, but I worry we'd be optimizing for fund mechanics over actual climate outcomes.

My current thinking: we need a barbell approach. A few deep-tech bets with 10+ year horizons where we can be patient capital, balanced against faster-moving AI infrastructure deals that generate the returns to justify that patience.

The LP base we've built should support this, but I want to stress-test the portfolio construction math again before our April offsite. Need to model what happens if the deep-tech positions take 18 months longer than projected.

Had a long call with [Anna Petrov](people/anna-petrov) today about their orange-mode implementation. I've been skeptical of these adaptive compute frameworks—too many startups slap "dynamic resource allocation" on a pitch deck without understanding the thermal constraints of real deployments.

But Anna walked me through their edge case handling, and I'm genuinely impressed. The way they throttle inference loads during peak grid stress isn't just clever engineering; it's the kind of grid-aware thinking that makes AI infrastructure actually deployable at scale without becoming another emissions nightmare.

She mentioned they're seeing 34% reduction in peak power draw during orange-mode events, which tracks with what we've modeled internally. The question I keep circling back to: can this approach generalize beyond their current data center partnerships, or is it too dependent on specific utility relationships?

Going to push for a deeper technical diligence session. If the architecture holds, this could reshape how we think about AI compute sustainability.

This week felt like running uphill with weights on. Three partner meetings, two LP calls, and somehow I still didn't get to half the founder emails sitting in my inbox.

The [Derek](people/derek-chen) conversation on Thursday keeps replaying in my head. His compute allocation thesis for inference workloads is sharper than I initially gave him credit for. Need to revisit my notes before our follow-up.

[Lina](people/lina-mwangi) flagged that our climate-AI overlap deals are getting more competitive—she's seeing later-stage firms creeping into Series A rounds we used to have to ourselves. That's both validating and annoying.

On the personal side: I missed two gym sessions and ate lunch at my desk four days straight. Not sustainable. [Toby](people/toby-fletcher) keeps reminding me that burnout doesn't announce itself, it just arrives. He's right.

Next week's priority: carve out actual thinking time. Block two mornings, no meetings. Let's see if I can actually protect them this time.

Woke up before my alarm today—5:47 AM, gray light already pushing through the curtains. Made coffee and sat with the quiet for a while. These mornings are rare now.

I've been thinking about pace. The fund moves fast, the founders move faster, and somewhere in between I'm supposed to find clarity. But clarity doesn't come from speed. It comes from stillness, from the gaps between meetings where I can actually hear my own thinking.

Three years ago I was grinding through diligence decks at 2 AM, convinced that was the job. Now I understand the job is judgment, and judgment requires rest. Requires space. Requires mornings like this one where I don't check Slack until after I've finished my cup.

The climate portfolio is at an inflection point. Several companies approaching Series B decisions simultaneously. I need to show up sharp for them, which means protecting these margins of silence when I can find them.

Had my weekly 1-1 with [Diego](people/diego-alvarez) this afternoon. He's been grinding hard on the Solaris deal and I can tell he's stretched thin—he mentioned working past midnight three times this week to get the technical DD wrapped up.

I pushed him a bit on his thesis around their cooling architecture. He defended it well, actually better than I expected. The kid has good instincts for separating marketing noise from genuine technical differentiation. That's rare in someone two years out of grad school.

But I'm worried about sustainability. I've seen this pattern before—junior investors burning hot for 18 months then flaming out. Told him to take Friday afternoon off after we close this phase. He seemed almost confused by the suggestion, which honestly concerns me more.

Need to be more intentional about modeling balance. Hard when I'm also running on fumes, but that's the job of a mentor—show them the pace is a marathon, not a sprint.

Spent most of today prepping for the Meridian board meeting next Tuesday. [Elena Voskresenskaya](people/elena-voskresenskaya) sent over the updated cap table, and I want to make sure I have our position straight before we walk in—Halfway holds 14.2% post-Series B, which gives us meaningful leverage on the expansion vote.

Called [Marcus Thompson](people/marcus-thompson-hw) to align on messaging around the compute partnership. He's more bullish than I am on the timeline, but we agreed to push for quarterly milestone reviews rather than the semi-annual cadence the founders proposed.

I keep coming back to the governance question. If we greenlight the infrastructure buildout in Southeast Asia, we need stronger reporting mechanisms. The climate impact story is compelling, but I've seen too many portfolio companies lose discipline when they scale fast.

Need to finalize my notes on the CFO transition before Monday. That conversation won't be comfortable.

Spent the morning reviewing our Q2 sourcing queue with [Derek](people/derek-huang). We have 47 companies flagged for initial outreach, but I'm noticing a pattern—too many are clustered around the same carbon accounting thesis we've already deployed into twice.

I asked Derek to re-weight toward grid flexibility and demand response. The regulatory tailwinds there are stronger than people realize, especially with FERC's recent signals on distributed resource aggregation.

[Priya](people/priya-anand) forwarded three inbound decks from her Stanford network. One caught my attention: a team building inference optimization specifically for weather prediction models. Interesting wedge—could reduce compute costs for forecasting by 40% if their benchmarks hold. Worth a call.

Note to self: we need to be more disciplined about tracking where our deals actually originate. Too much is still coming through warm intros rather than proactive thesis-driven hunting. That worked at $50M fund size. At $200M, we need systems.

Had a long call with [Kofi](people/kofi-mensah) this morning about structuring the threshold terms for the Heliogen follow-on. He's pushing for a lower revenue milestone before the second tranche unlocks—$4M ARR instead of our standard $6M for climate infrastructure deals at this stage.

His argument isn't unreasonable. The regulatory tailwinds from the IRA extension mean these companies are hitting inflection points faster than our 2023-era models predicted. If we hold to $6M, we might be leaving relationship capital on the table when founders feel we're being unnecessarily conservative.

But I keep coming back to the same concern: threshold terms exist to protect deployment discipline, not just our downside. When we lower bars, we're also lowering the operational pressure that forces founding teams to prove unit economics before scaling.

Going to suggest a compromise—$5M ARR but with a 90-day trailing requirement instead of point-in-time measurement. Forces consistency without punishing reasonable seasonality. Will draft the term sheet language tonight.

Just finished digesting the Q1 climate-tech market report that [Marcus Chen](people/marcus-chen) circulated this morning. The numbers are more encouraging than I expected—grid-scale storage deployments up 34% YoY, and the AI-for-energy-optimization segment is finally showing real traction beyond pilot programs.

What caught my attention: [Elena Vasquez](people/elena-vasquez) is still CEO at Terraform Dynamics, and under her leadership they've captured nearly 18% of the predictive maintenance market for renewable assets. That's a remarkable position given they only closed their Series B fourteen months ago. We passed on that round—something I've been second-guessing since November.

The report confirms my thesis that inference-heavy climate applications are hitting an infrastructure ceiling. Compute costs remain the bottleneck. Our portfolio companies keep asking about dedicated AI infra for sustainability workloads.

Need to schedule a deeper dive with Marcus next week. If the tailwinds hold through Q2, we should accelerate our fund deployment timeline.

Spent most of today deep in diligence on [Jordan Melo](people/jordan-melo)'s background before we finalize the [Terraflux](companies/terraflux) term sheet. Third reference call confirmed what I suspected—Jordan's technical chops are real, but the more interesting signal is how former reports talk about them. Two used the word "relentless" unprompted. One mentioned Jordan once debugged a carbon accounting discrepancy at 2am on Christmas Eve because "the numbers have to be right."

That's the founder energy I look for. Climate infrastructure doesn't forgive sloppiness—grid operators, utilities, regulators will eat you alive if you can't back up your claims with airtight data.

Still want to dig into the co-founder dynamic more. [Jordan](people/jordan-melo) clearly drives the technical vision, but I need to understand how decisions get made when they disagree. Planning to grab coffee with their CTO next week to get a read on that chemistry. Partnership health is the thing that kills companies quietly.

Q2 planning feels more consequential this year. We're sitting on dry powder while half the market is still recovering from the correction, and I keep coming back to the same question: do we lean into the fear or wait for clearer signals?

Had a good call with [Lena Park](people/lena-park) yesterday about her team's pipeline. She's seeing interesting deal flow in grid optimization and industrial electrification—areas where the regulatory tailwinds are finally catching up to the technology. Her instinct is to move faster on the Series A opportunities before the larger funds finish licking their wounds and crowd back in.

I'm inclined to agree. Our thesis has always been that climate infrastructure needs patient capital, but patience doesn't mean passivity. If we can close two to three deals by end of June at reasonable valuations, we'll be well-positioned regardless of where macro goes.

Need to sync with the full team next week and pressure-test the reserve allocation model. Don't want to overcommit early and regret it by Q4.

Finally had the follow-up with [Chen Wei](people/chen-wei) from [Novamind](companies/novamind) this afternoon. The technical deep-dive was more impressive than I expected—their approach to inference optimization for edge deployments is genuinely novel, not just incremental improvement on existing architectures.

What struck me most was Chen's clarity about where they're *not* competing. He's explicitly avoiding the hyperscaler collision course that killed three similar startups last year. Instead, they're targeting industrial IoT and distributed grid monitoring—exactly where our climate thesis intersects with AI infrastructure.

Still have concerns about their go-to-market timeline. They're projecting enterprise contracts by Q3, but the sales team is thin. I pushed on this and Chen acknowledged they need a VP of Sales with utility sector experience. Said he'd prioritize the hire.

I'm leaning toward recommending we lead the Series A, but want to see their pilot data from the Texas grid project before presenting to the partnership. Will ask [Chen Wei](people/chen-wei) for access next week.

Spent the morning revisiting our climate thesis with [David Chen](people/david-chen) and I keep coming back to the same tension: the most impactful interventions in carbon removal and grid optimization are increasingly dependent on AI infrastructure that barely exists at scale.

We're not just investing in climate anymore—we're betting on the convergence. The founders who understand both the thermodynamics *and* the transformer architectures are vanishingly rare, but they're the ones building decade-defining companies.

[Maria Santos](people/maria-santos) pushed back on this framing last week, arguing we're overcomplicating the thesis. Maybe. But every deal I've seen in the last quarter that genuinely excited me sat at this intersection: adaptive load balancing, satellite-driven MRV, process optimization for green hydrogen.

I need to write this up more formally for the LP update. The "climate + AI infra" positioning felt contrarian eighteen months ago. Now it feels obvious, which probably means we're entering a crowded phase. Time to sharpen our edge.

Had a long call with [Elena Rossi](people/elena-rossi) this morning about what she's calling "orange-mode" operations at GridSync. Basically a middle state between normal ops and full emergency protocols—when the grid is stressed but not yet failing. She's convinced this is where the real value creation happens for AI-managed systems.

I keep turning this over. Most founders pitch me on either the steady-state efficiency gains or the crisis-response heroics. Elena's focused on the liminal space. The hours before a blackout that never happens because the system anticipated and adapted.

It's harder to quantify, which makes it harder to sell to LPs. But I think she's onto something fundamental about how resilient infrastructure actually works. Prevention doesn't make headlines, but it's where the compounding value lives.

Need to dig into their telemetry data from the February cold snap. If the numbers support the thesis, this could reshape how we evaluate the whole portfolio's climate resilience stack.
